attributes.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic a referral for an outpatient CT scan of her abdomen with contrast ordered by an unknown other specialist. She has a medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, asthma, hypertension, and stage 4 chronic kidney disease. Her medications include metformin, perindopril, alvesco, ventolin and insulin glargine. Her estimated glomerular filtration rate is 25 mL/min/1.73m2 (>60). She reports having had a previous CT scan two years ago complicated by severe bronchospasm requiring emergency treatment with adrenaline.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is an absolute contraindication to having a CT scan with contrast?
options: Previous severe radiocontrast media hypersensitivity reaction requiring emergency treatment
Estimated glomerular filtration rate less than 30 mL/min/1.73m2
attributes.explanation:
When considering contrast administration for CT imaging, it is crucial to distinguish between absolute and relative contraindications. Previous severe contrast reaction requiring emergency treatment is an absolute contraindication to receiving the same contrast agent due to the high risk of an even more severe, potentially life-threatening reaction.

An estimated glomerular filtration rate less than 30 mL/min/1.73m2 is an important relative contraindication due to the increased risk of contrast-induced nephropathy. However, in some clinical scenarios, the benefits of contrast imaging may outweigh this risk with appropriate precautions thus is a not an absolute contraindication.

Current metformin therapy is a relative contraindication that can be managed by temporarily withholding the medication for 48 hours after contrast administration to prevent lactic acidosis, particularly in patients with renal impairment.

Potential pregnancy is not a contraindication to CT with contrast, although radiation exposure should be minimised where possible. The benefits and risks should be carefully weighed.

Asthma is a relative contraindication that requires careful consideration but is not absolute. While asthmatics may be at higher risk of contrast reactions, many can safely receive contrast with appropriate preparation and monitoring.

Understanding these distinctions is crucial for safe imaging practice. While relative contraindications can often be managed with appropriate precautions, absolute contraindications represent situations where the risk is deemed unacceptable regardless of potential benefits.
learningPoints: Previous severe contrast reaction requiring emergency treatment is an absolute contraindication to the same contrast agent.
Relative contraindications to contrast include renal impairment and metformin therapy.
Premedication may reduce risk in mild-moderate previous reactions but is not appropriate for severe reactions.
references: Radiocontrast Media Hypersensitivity. Australia Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy. 2022. Available at: https://www.allergy.org.au/hp/drug-allergy/radiocontrast-media

Hsieh C, Wu SC, Kosik RO, Huang YC, Chan WP. Pharmacological Prevention of Hypersensitivity Reactions Caused by Iodinated Contrast Media: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Diagnostics (Basel). 2022 Jul 9;12(7):1673. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9320945/
